description: Analytical structure families for business, marketing, and financial analysis. Each family defines a canonical way to lay out evidence so that a specific class of question becomes answerable.
usage: Select a structure after identifying the paradigm. The structure makes the paradigm concrete: it fixes what columns, rows, nodes, or cells must be filled in and what the answer actually looks like.

families.name: Driver Tree
families.coreContract: A hierarchical decomposition of a top-level metric into multiplicative or additive drivers, each of which can be estimated or measured separately.
whenToPrefer: The question is 'why is X the value it is' or 'what moves X the most'
A single metric (revenue, margin, LTV) needs to be explained or forecast
Root-cause analysis across an aggregate KPI

variants.name: Revenue Tree
variants.characteristic: Revenue = customers × ARPU, further split into acquisition, retention, pricing, and mix.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Diagnosing or projecting a top-line number.

variants.name: DuPont Decomposition
variants.characteristic: ROE = margin × turnover × leverage; exposes which lever drives profitability.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Explaining a change in return on equity between periods or peers.

variants.name: Unit Economics Tree
variants.characteristic: Contribution = price − variable cost, rolled up through volume and fixed cost.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Deciding whether a product or segment is structurally profitable.

implementationTraps: Mixing additive and multiplicative levels without marking them
Letting branches overlap so a change double-counts
Expanding the tree deeper on the convenient side, not the uncertain side
disqualifiers: The question is comparative across discrete alternatives, not decompositional
The metric has no stable causal decomposition (pure market sentiment, for example)

families.name: Decision Matrix (Weighted Scoring)
families.coreContract: Options as rows, weighted criteria as columns; each cell is a score, and the weighted sum produces a ranking.
whenToPrefer: A discrete choice among three or more comparable options
Multiple criteria must be traded off transparently
Stakeholders need to see how weights drive the conclusion

variants.name: Unweighted Pugh Matrix
variants.characteristic: Score each option vs. a baseline with +/0/−, no weights.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Early-stage option screening where precision is false comfort.

variants.name: Weighted Scorecard
variants.characteristic: Numeric weights on each criterion sum to 1; scores are 1–5 or 1–10.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Vendor selection, hire/no-hire panels, make-vs-buy comparisons.

variants.name: AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process)
variants.characteristic: Pairwise comparisons derive weights with consistency checks.
variants.bestUsedWhen: High-stakes decisions where weight-elicitation bias must be disciplined.

implementationTraps: Back-fitting weights until the preferred option wins
Including correlated criteria that double-count the same underlying factor
Scoring on scales that are not anchored to observable behavior
disqualifiers: A single dominant criterion swamps all others (then it is not a matrix question)
Option set is continuous rather than discrete

families.name: Porter Five Forces Grid
families.coreContract: Five canonical force categories — rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, buyer power, supplier power — each scored with evidence and a direction of pressure.
whenToPrefer: Assessing structural attractiveness of an industry or segment
Explaining persistent margin pressure
Framing entry, exit, or reposition decisions

variants.name: Classic Five Forces
variants.characteristic: Qualitative evidence under each of the five force headings.
variants.bestUsedWhen: A narrative industry assessment aimed at senior stakeholders.

variants.name: Scored Five Forces
variants.characteristic: Each force rated Low/Medium/High with explicit drivers.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Comparing attractiveness across multiple industries in a portfolio screen.

variants.name: Six Forces (with Complementors)
variants.characteristic: Adds complementors as a sixth force for platform and ecosystem dynamics.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Platform, marketplace, or ecosystem industries where complements drive value.

implementationTraps: Treating the grid as a checklist rather than a causal explanation of profits
Ignoring substitutes outside the traditional industry definition
Confusing a current snapshot with the direction of change
disqualifiers: Question is about a single company's execution, not industry structure
Market is too nascent to have stable force dynamics

families.name: SWOT Matrix
families.coreContract: A 2x2 of Strengths/Weaknesses (internal) vs Opportunities/Threats (external), used to identify strategic moves that leverage or mitigate each quadrant.
whenToPrefer: Positioning review for a business unit or product
Workshop setting where shared context must be built quickly
Bridge between internal capability audit and external environment scan

variants.name: Classic SWOT
variants.characteristic: Bullet lists inside each of the four quadrants.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Initial framing with leadership teams.

variants.name: TOWS Matrix
variants.characteristic: Cross-quadrant combinations (e.g., Strength × Opportunity) yield specific strategic actions.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Turning a SWOT inventory into an action plan.

variants.name: Weighted SWOT
variants.characteristic: Each item is rated on impact and probability to prioritize focus.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Follow-up prioritization after a classic SWOT has generated a long list.

implementationTraps: Generating ungrounded bullet lists that feel comprehensive but drive no action
Confusing strengths with things the team is merely proud of
Miscategorizing an internal weakness as an external threat
disqualifiers: Quantitative precision is required — SWOT is framing, not measurement
Decision is between two specific options (use decision-matrix instead)

families.name: Cohort Table
families.coreContract: Rows are cohorts defined by an acquisition event (month, campaign, channel); columns are time-since-acquisition; cells are retention, revenue, or another behavior metric.
whenToPrefer: Retention, LTV, or payback analysis for subscription or repeat-purchase businesses
Evaluating the durability of recent product or marketing changes
Separating signal from mix shift when the customer base is growing

variants.name: Retention Cohort
variants.characteristic: Cells = % of cohort still active at month N.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Diagnosing churn dynamics and steady-state retention.

variants.name: Revenue Cohort
variants.characteristic: Cells = revenue per cohort member at month N.
variants.bestUsedWhen: LTV estimation and payback-period analysis.

variants.name: Channel / Campaign Cohort
variants.characteristic: Cohorts defined by acquisition source to compare quality, not just quantity.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Deciding which acquisition channels to scale or defund.

implementationTraps: Comparing young cohorts to mature ones without adjusting for incomplete data
Pooling across channels so a mix shift masquerades as a retention change
Reading a single anomalous row as a trend
disqualifiers: Customers are one-time, non-repeat purchasers with no ongoing relationship
Acquisition event is not well-defined (B2B with long diffuse sales cycles)

families.name: Comparables Table
families.coreContract: Rows are peer entities (companies, assets, deals); columns are standardized financial and operating metrics and multiples; output is a benchmark range for the subject.
whenToPrefer: Relative valuation of a public or private company
Sanity-check on a DCF output
Benchmarking operating metrics across peers

variants.name: Trading Comparables
variants.characteristic: Listed peers, current multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/Sales).
variants.bestUsedWhen: Market-based valuation for a public or IPO-bound company.

variants.name: Transaction Comparables
variants.characteristic: Historical M&A deals for similar assets, with deal multiples.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Pricing a strategic acquisition or sell-side process.

variants.name: Operating Benchmarks
variants.characteristic: Non-valuation metrics: gross margin, ARR growth, CAC payback, churn.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Board benchmarking and performance diagnostics.

implementationTraps: Peer set selected to support a conclusion rather than for true comparability
Using headline multiples without adjusting for capital structure or accounting differences
Outliers dominating the mean — use medians and trimmed ranges
disqualifiers: No true peer set exists (novel business model)
Question requires an intrinsic answer independent of market sentiment

families.name: Cost-Benefit Ledger
families.coreContract: A two-column accounting of quantified costs and benefits (one-time and ongoing), netted to a return, payback period, or NPV.
whenToPrefer: Go/no-go on a discrete investment or initiative
Justifying internal projects to a finance or steering committee
Pricing-change or process-change evaluation

variants.name: Simple Payback
variants.characteristic: Undiscounted cash flows; payback = investment ÷ annual benefit.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Small projects, short horizons, fast screening.

variants.name: NPV / IRR Ledger
variants.characteristic: Cash flows discounted at cost of capital; NPV and IRR computed.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Capital projects with multi-year horizons and non-trivial scale.

variants.name: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
variants.characteristic: Full lifecycle cost accounting including hidden and downstream costs.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Vendor, platform, or infrastructure decisions.

implementationTraps: Omitting cannibalization or opportunity cost on the benefit side
Ignoring ongoing support and retirement costs
Double-counting benefits that also appear as revenue in another business case
disqualifiers: Benefits are strategic and genuinely unquantifiable — ledger will mislead
Decision is about choosing among options (use decision-matrix instead)

families.name: Scenario Grid
families.coreContract: A small set of internally consistent scenarios (often 3 or a 2x2) against which a strategy, forecast, or portfolio is evaluated.
whenToPrefer: Plan needs to be stress-tested across high-uncertainty futures
Capital or strategy commitments are irreversible
Robustness, not optimality, is the objective

variants.name: Base / Bull / Bear
variants.characteristic: Three scenarios on a coordinated set of drivers.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Annual plans, earnings guidance, investment memos.

variants.name: 2x2 Scenario Matrix
variants.characteristic: Two axes of uncertainty crossed into four named futures.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Long-horizon strategy with two dominant exogenous uncertainties.

variants.name: Stress / Tail Scenario
variants.characteristic: Deliberate low-probability, high-severity case.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Risk management, regulatory capital, and business-continuity planning.

implementationTraps: Scenarios that are just point estimates ±10% with no narrative difference
Internally inconsistent drivers (e.g., booming demand and falling prices together) with no rationale
Failing to define decision actions that differ across scenarios
disqualifiers: Decision timeframe is short and uncertainty is bounded
A single-point forecast is sufficient for the decision

families.name: Sensitivity Tornado
families.coreContract: Horizontal bars showing the change in an output metric when each input is flexed through a defined range, sorted by impact magnitude.
whenToPrefer: Identifying which inputs actually drive a model's output
Communicating model risk to non-technical stakeholders
Prioritizing which assumptions deserve deeper research

variants.name: One-Way Tornado
variants.characteristic: Flex one input at a time between low and high; bar shows output range.
variants.bestUsedWhen: First-pass sensitivity on any quantitative model.

variants.name: Two-Way Sensitivity Table
variants.characteristic: Two inputs varied simultaneously in a grid.
variants.bestUsedWhen: When two drivers are suspected to interact (growth × margin).

variants.name: Monte Carlo Sensitivity
variants.characteristic: Inputs sampled jointly from distributions; contribution to variance reported.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Models with many uncertain inputs and non-linear interactions.

implementationTraps: Flex ranges that are arbitrarily symmetric rather than reflecting actual uncertainty
Treating the tornado as a forecast instead of a diagnostic
Ignoring correlations between inputs
disqualifiers: Output is qualitative — no numeric metric to flex
Model is so simple that sensitivity is obvious by inspection

families.name: DCF Model
families.coreContract: An explicit projection of free cash flows over a finite horizon plus a terminal value, each discounted to present value at a risk-adjusted rate.
whenToPrefer: Intrinsic valuation of a company, project, or asset
Decisions requiring a fair-value anchor independent of current market prices
Long-duration cash-flow assets (infrastructure, real estate, subscription businesses)

variants.name: Unlevered FCF DCF
variants.characteristic: Value the enterprise using free cash flow to the firm, discount at WACC.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Standard corporate valuation with a mixed capital structure.

variants.name: Levered / Equity DCF
variants.characteristic: Value equity directly using free cash flow to equity and cost of equity.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Financial institutions or highly levered companies.

variants.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
variants.characteristic: Value as all-equity, then add present value of tax shield and other financing effects.
variants.bestUsedWhen: Transactions with changing capital structure (LBOs, project finance).

implementationTraps: Terminal value dominating the answer with no sensitivity reporting
Discount rate chosen by convention rather than derived from the asset's risk
Projecting growth above GDP into perpetuity
disqualifiers: Cash flows are too speculative to project meaningfully (very early-stage startups)
A market-based relative valuation is clearly more defensible

crossStructureDecisionGuide.bottleneckOperation: Explain or decompose a single aggregate metric
Choose one from a small set of discrete options with multiple criteria
Assess industry or segment structural profitability
Build shared framing of internal vs external position
Analyze retention, LTV, or time-based customer behavior
Benchmark valuation or operating metrics against peers
Justify a discrete investment with quantified return
Stress-test a plan against deep uncertainty
Identify which assumptions dominate a quantitative model
Produce an intrinsic fair value independent of market prices
